text.title: recruiting-process Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for recruiting-process. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Use at least three sourcing channels per role; over-reliance on a single channel (e.g., job boards) caps the addressable candidate pool and biases toward active seekers.
rule.rationale: LinkedIn data shows 70% of the global workforce is passively open to opportunities; job boards only reach active seekers, typically 30% of the market.

rule.statement: Remove degree requirements that are not job-essential; replace with skills assessments or portfolio evaluation.
rule.rationale: 76% of organizations report successful hires from candidates previously excluded by degree requirements (LinkedIn). Degree proxies exclude qualified candidates from under-resourced educational backgrounds without improving quality-of-hire.

rule.statement: Each outreach message must reference the candidate's specific work; generic "exciting opportunity" subject lines achieve less than 10% open rate.
rule.rationale: Passive candidates receive multiple generic outreach messages weekly. A specific reference to a project, article, or contribution they produced proves the sender did research and respects their time.

rule.statement: Build and maintain warm candidate lists for critical roles before those roles open; reactive hiring from a cold start adds 2-4 weeks to time-to-fill.
rule.rationale: Pipeline building converts recruiting from a reactive sprint into an ongoing relationship. When a role opens, the first outreach goes to candidates who already know the company.

rule.statement: Post all roles internally before external sourcing begins; an internal hire closes 50-70% faster and retains institutional knowledge.
rule.rationale: Internal candidates require less ramp time, already understand culture, and their move opens a backfill at a more junior level — cheaper to fill. Skipping internal posting destroys trust in career development commitments.

rule.statement: Limit must-have requirements to 5-7 items and nice-to-have to 3-4; JDs listing 15+ requirements reduce qualified applicant volume without improving hire quality.
rule.rationale: Research (LinkedIn, Textio) shows that long requirement lists disproportionately deter underrepresented candidates who self-screen out if they don't meet every requirement. Reducing to true requirements increases qualified applicant volume.

rule.statement: Define the target candidate persona before writing the JD or opening sourcing channels; without a profile, sourcing generates noise and screening becomes subjective.
rule.rationale: A persona converts the hiring manager's vague "someone like our best performer" into specific, sourceable attributes: experience range, current role, motivations, channels. This makes sourcing targeted and screening criteria explicit.

rule.statement: Report six core metrics weekly or monthly; any metric without a defined target and owner degrades into a vanity number.
rule.rationale: Metrics without targets drive reporting behavior, not improvement. Assigning ownership (recruiter for time-to-fill, compensation for offer acceptance) creates accountability.

rule.statement: Application process must complete in under 5 minutes and render correctly on mobile; test with a real resume before publishing every role.
rule.rationale: ATS default forms often include non-essential fields (graduation year, GPA, references before interview) that extend application time and reduce conversion. Mobile rendering issues silently drop applicants who don't self-report the problem.

rule.statement: Audit every AI sourcing or screening tool for demographic bias before deployment; document the audit and review annually.
rule.rationale: AI tools trained on historical hiring data replicate historical bias patterns. EEOC and EU AI Act require explainability for decisions affecting employment; undocumented AI use creates legal exposure.

rule.statement: Extend the offer within 48 hours of the hiring decision; every additional day increases competing-offer risk by a measurable margin.
rule.rationale: Top candidates in active searches often have multiple processes running in parallel. A 5-day lag between decision and offer letter loses 20-30% of candidates in competitive markets.
